items.title: Project Lifecycle Phases
items.description: A project typically progresses through a sequence of phases: initiation (defining purpose, sponsor, and high-level scope), planning (detailing scope, schedule, cost, resources, and risk), execution (producing deliverables), monitoring and controlling (tracking variance against the baseline and applying corrective action), and closing (formal acceptance, handover, and release of resources). Phases may be sequential (predictive/waterfall), overlapping, or iterative (adaptive/agile), but every approach still passes through the underlying logical groups of work. Phase boundaries are natural checkpoints where deliverables are reviewed and a go/no-go decision is made before committing further investment.
items.applicability: Use to frame any structured delivery effort and to decide where stage gates, reviews, and funding decisions should sit. Applies to both predictive and adaptive delivery, with iterations replacing single execution passes in agile contexts.
items.example: A banking middleware upgrade moves from initiation (charter approved by the sponsor) to planning (baselined schedule and risk register), through execution sprints, with monitoring via weekly status, and closing once UAT sign-off and production handover are complete.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Scope Management and Creep Control
items.description: Scope management defines and controls what is and is not included in the project, captured in a scope statement and decomposed into a work breakdown structure (WBS) so deliverables are explicit and testable. Scope creep is the uncontrolled, incremental expansion of scope without corresponding adjustments to time, cost, or quality, usually arising from informal requests that bypass change control. The defence is a clear baseline, an exclusions list, and a rule that any addition must pass through formal change control with re-estimation and re-approval. Distinguish scope creep (unmanaged) from legitimate, approved scope change.
items.applicability: Apply whenever requirements are negotiated or deliverables defined, and any time stakeholders request 'just one more thing' mid-flight. Essential for fixed-price and regulated work where uncontrolled additions erode margin and timelines.
items.example: A request to add a new report to an in-flight integration is logged, estimated at five days, and routed to change control rather than quietly absorbed; the sponsor approves the cost and the schedule baseline is updated.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Risk Register and Response Planning
items.description: A risk register is the living record of identified threats and opportunities, each with a description, category, owner, probability, impact, resulting severity score, and a planned response. Common threat responses are avoid, mitigate, transfer, and accept; for opportunities they are exploit, enhance, share, and accept, with residual risk and contingency reserves tracked after the response. Risks are reviewed on a regular cadence so new risks are added, closed risks retired, and triggers monitored. Issues differ from risks: a risk is a future possibility, an issue is a problem that has already occurred.
items.applicability: Use throughout the lifecycle to make uncertainty visible and assign accountability for managing it. Particularly valuable in regulated banking delivery where audit trails of risk decisions are expected.
items.example: Risk: 'Third-party Parascript licence renewal may slip past go-live.' Probability medium, impact high, owner the vendor manager, response mitigate by starting renewal 90 days early, with a fallback contingency of a temporary licence extension.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Stakeholder Matrix and Engagement
items.description: A stakeholder matrix identifies everyone affected by or able to affect the project and classifies them so engagement effort is proportionate, most commonly along power and interest axes (manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor). Each stakeholder is profiled by influence, expectations, communication needs, and current versus desired level of engagement. The analysis feeds the communication plan and helps surface hidden blockers early. It is revisited as the project evolves, since influence and attitude shift over time.
items.applicability: Use at initiation and at each major phase to plan communication, secure buy-in, and pre-empt resistance. Critical when a delivery spans multiple business units, vendors, and regulatory bodies.
items.example: The risk officer is high-power/high-interest and managed closely with weekly briefings, while an adjacent team lead is low-power/high-interest and simply kept informed via the monthly newsletter.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Acceptance Criteria
items.description: Acceptance criteria are the predefined, testable conditions a deliverable must satisfy to be accepted by the customer or sponsor, converting subjective expectations into objective, verifiable statements. Good criteria are specific, measurable, unambiguous, and agreed before work begins, so 'done' is not negotiated after the fact. They are often expressed in a Given/When/Then form or as a checklist, and they form the basis for test cases and formal sign-off. They operate at multiple levels: per user story, per deliverable, and per overall project (definition of done).
items.applicability: Define for every requirement, story, or deliverable before development starts, and reference them at review and sign-off. They anchor UAT and protect against scope disputes.
items.example: Given a cheque image with a printed amount, when the OCR pipeline processes it, then the recognised amount must match the printed value for at least 98 percent of a defined test set before the feature is accepted.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Issue Log
items.description: An issue log is the central record of problems that have already materialised and require resolution to keep the project on track, distinct from the risk register which tracks future possibilities. Each entry captures a unique identifier, description, date raised, raiser, owner, priority or severity, status, target resolution date, and the eventual resolution. Reviewing the log on a regular cadence ensures issues are actively driven to closure rather than left to fester. Aging and recurring issues are escalation signals that something systemic needs attention.
items.applicability: Maintain continuously during execution and monitoring; review in every status meeting and escalate blockers per the governance path. Useful evidence in audits and post-implementation reviews.
items.example: Issue #14: 'Test environment database refresh failing nightly.' Raised by QA, owned by the DBA, priority high, status in-progress, target resolution in three days, blocking regression testing until fixed.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Decision Log
items.description: A decision log records significant decisions, the context and options considered, the rationale, who made the decision, and the date, creating an auditable history that prevents rehashing settled questions. It complements but is distinct from issue and risk logs: it captures the conclusions reached rather than open problems. Capturing rationale matters because the people and reasoning behind a choice are often forgotten months later. For architecture-level choices, an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a closely related lightweight format.
items.applicability: Use whenever a non-trivial, hard-to-reverse, or cross-team choice is made. Especially valuable on long-running estates where staff turnover erodes institutional memory.
items.example: Decision #7: 'Use SQL Server as the sole required datastore; Qdrant and Ollama remain optional.' Rationale: must run in environments with no GPU or internet. Decided by the architecture lead, with two rejected alternatives noted.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Change Control Board
items.description: A Change Control Board (CCB), sometimes called a change advisory board, is the authorised group that evaluates and approves or rejects change requests against the project baselines. Its process is to log each request, assess impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk, then decide and update baselines and the configuration record accordingly. The CCB provides a single, transparent gate so changes are deliberate rather than ad hoc, and it sizes its formality to the value and risk of the change. Approved changes flow into re-planning; rejected ones are recorded with reasons.
items.applicability: Stand up whenever baselines are formally controlled, particularly on regulated, multi-vendor, or fixed-price work. The board's authority and quorum should be defined in the governance plan.
items.example: A request to extend retention of ingested audit data from 90 to 365 days is submitted to the CCB, which assesses storage cost and compliance benefit, approves it, and triggers an updated cost baseline and migration task.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: RACI Responsibility Matrix
items.description: A RACI matrix maps activities or deliverables against roles, assigning each a designation of Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome and signs off), Consulted (provides input through two-way dialogue), or Informed (kept up to date one-way). A healthy matrix has exactly one Accountable per activity to avoid diffused ownership, and it limits Responsible and Consulted entries to keep work tractable. It clarifies who does what across teams and is a fast way to surface gaps (no owner) or overload (too many As). Variants such as RASCI add a Support role.
items.applicability: Use during planning and at handover boundaries to remove ambiguity over ownership, particularly across vendor and business-unit lines. Revisit when roles or org structure change.
items.example: For 'production deployment': the release engineer is Responsible, the delivery lead is Accountable, the security officer and DBA are Consulted, and the business sponsor is Informed.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Lessons Learned
items.description: Lessons learned capture what went well, what did not, and what should change, so future projects benefit from accumulated experience rather than repeating mistakes. The practice is most effective when gathered continuously throughout delivery, not only at closure, and when each lesson is concrete, attributed to a cause, and paired with a recommended action. To create real value the repository must be searchable and actively consulted at the start of new work, otherwise it becomes a write-only archive. Lessons feed organisational process improvement and updates to standards and checklists.
items.applicability: Capture at phase ends, after incidents, and at closure; consult at initiation and planning of new projects. Especially valuable across a long-lived estate of related legacy systems.
items.example: Lesson: 'Vendor licence renewals consistently slip; start renewal 90 days early.' Cause: late vendor engagement on a prior project. Action: added to the standard project checklist and the risk register template.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Status Reporting
items.description: Status reporting communicates project health to stakeholders on a regular cadence, typically covering overall RAG (red/amber/green) status, progress against milestones, budget and schedule variance, key risks and issues, decisions needed, and the outlook for the next period. Effective reports are concise, audience-tailored, honest about problems, and consistent over time so trends are visible. They distinguish information (for awareness) from escalations (requiring a decision), and they avoid burying a red status inside reassuring narrative. The format scales from a one-page executive dashboard to a detailed team report.
items.applicability: Use on every active project at a fixed cadence; tailor depth to the audience (steering committee versus delivery team). The cadence and distribution list are defined in the communication plan.
items.example: A weekly one-pager shows overall status amber, scope on track, schedule slipping two days due to environment issues (issue #14), top risk the vendor licence renewal, and one decision requested from the sponsor on test-data sourcing.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Quality Gates and Deployment Readiness
items.description: A quality gate is a defined checkpoint at which work must meet explicit, agreed criteria before it may proceed to the next stage, preventing defects and unfinished work from flowing downstream. Deployment readiness is the final gate before release, typically verified by a go-live checklist covering passed tests, completed UAT sign-off, security and performance validation, documented rollback plan, capacity and monitoring readiness, and stakeholder approval. Gates are most effective when their pass criteria are objective and decided in advance, and when a failed gate genuinely halts progress rather than being waived under pressure. A go/no-go meeting formalises the decision with named approvers.
items.applicability: Apply at phase transitions and immediately before any production deployment, especially in regulated banking environments where release controls are audited. Tailor checklist rigour to the change's risk.
items.example: Before production release, the go/no-go checklist confirms regression suite green, UAT signed off, a tested rollback script, monitoring dashboards live, and the change approved by the CCB; one open security finding flips the gate to no-go.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.

items.title: Post-Implementation Review
items.description: A post-implementation review (PIR), or post-project review, is conducted after a deliverable has been live for a meaningful period to assess whether the project achieved its intended outcomes and realised the expected benefits. It compares actual results against the original business case and objectives, evaluates delivery performance, confirms support and operational handover are working, and consolidates lessons learned. Running it after enough time has elapsed for benefits to appear distinguishes it from a closure meeting held at handover. The output feeds benefits tracking, accountability, and organisational improvement.
items.applicability: Schedule weeks to months after go-live, once steady-state operation provides real data on benefits and stability. Pair with the lessons-learned repository and the original business case.
items.example: Three months after launching the cheque-recognition upgrade, a PIR confirms processing time fell as projected, two benefits were partially realised, support tickets stabilised, and three lessons were logged for the next estate modernisation.
items.limitation: This is a practical summary for working delivery leads and is not a substitute for the formal PMBOK Guide, an equivalent licensed standard, or professional project-management advice.
